Off Calendar Feb-13-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CARLOS CORVERA, M.D.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication By Carlos Uriel Corvera, M.D.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied.

On summary judgment/adjudication, "[t]he moving party's evidence must be strictly construed, while the opposing party's evidence must be liberally construed." (Binder v. Aetna Life Ins. Co. (1999) 75 Cal.App.4th 831, 838.)

Defendant's alternative motion for summary adjudication on the medical malpractice cause of action is denied. Defendant maintained its initial burden of production and shows that defendant did not breach the standard of care, nor did he cause injury to decedent. (Declaration of William C. Chapman, M.D., pars. 4, 24-32.)

When a defendant moves for summary judgment and supports its motion with an expert declaration that its conduct fell within the standard of care, the defendant is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence. (See Munro v. Regents of the University of California (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977.) In this case, plaintiff creates triable issues of fact as to breach and causation. (Declaration of Alagappan Annamalai, M.D., pars. 10-12.) "[T]he expert's declaration is to be liberally construed. [citation] We must resolve 'any doubts as to the propriety of granting the motion in favor of the plaintiff. [Citation.]'" (Jennifer C. v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1320, 1332.) The court notes that the separate statement shall include only material facts that relate to the cause of action and dispose of the motion, not mere background facts. (CRC 3.1350(a)(2); Beltran v. Hard Rock Hotel Licensing, Inc. (2023) 97 Cal.App.5th 865, 875.) Defendant's 320-fact separate statement does not comport with this rule.

Defendant's alternative motion for summary adjudication on cause of action two for violation of the Unruh Act is granted. "[A] plaintiff must . . . plead and prove a case of intentional discrimination to recover under the [Unruh] Act." (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1149 (superseded by statute with regard to disability claims).) Defendant declares that he harbored no discriminatory animus towards decedent. (Declaration of Dr. Covera, par. 2.) Defendant's expert confirms that decedent received full and equal accommodations. (Declaration of William C. Chapman, M.D., pars. 5, 33.) In opposition, plaintiff has agreed to "drop this cause of action." (Opposition, 11:1.) Summary adjudication is therefore granted.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

Off Calendar Sep-30-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Defendant Regents' demurrer to the second cause of action for violation of the Unruh Act is sustained with leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 430.10(e).) First, even though plaintiff alleges that she is decedent's surviving heir at paragraph 7 of the Complaint, she is apparently only bringing this action in her individual capacity. (Complaint, Caption, 2:9, 3:25-26.) Plaintiff has leave to amend to allege that she is bringing the Unruh Act claim as decedent's successor in interest. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 377.30.) Plaintiff shall also comply with the affidavit requirement of Code Civ. Proc. section 377.32. (See Aghaian v. Minassian (2021) 64 Cal.App.5th 603, 279 Cal.Rptr.3d 191, 202.)

Second, plaintiff has leave to allege facts regarding how defendant's alleged medical care derelictions were intentionally discriminatory. The current general allegations regarding alleged structural and implicit bias are insufficient. (See, e.g., Complaint, pars. 20-27, 40.) With the exception of ADA claims, the Unruh Act mandates that "[a plaintiff must . . . plead and prove a case of intentional discrimination to recover under the Act." (Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV (1991) 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1149 (superseded by statute on other grounds as stated in Munson v. Del Taco, Inc. (2009) 46 Cal.4th 661, 667-668.) With the exception of ADA claims, an Unruh Act claim cannot be premised on a disparate impact analysis because such an analysis is inconsistent with a finding of intentional discrimination. (Id. at 1172-1175.) The Court further notes that while the Unruh Act seeks to remedy discrimination, "it does not seek to remedy traditional wrongs arising out of tort or breach of contract . . . " (Frantz v. Blackwell (1987) 189 Cal.App.3d 91, 96.)

Order is signed
